sections.title: The Blue Planet
sections.content_summary: The section opens with astronaut Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla's reflection that Earth looks borderless from space, leading to the central theme that Earth is humanity's one shared home. It notes that from space only broad landmasses and oceans are visible, establishing the idea that national borders are human constructs invisible in nature. A 'Do you know?' box introduces Wing Commander Rakesh Sharma's famous reply 'Saare Jahaan Se Achcha' when asked how India looked from space. A second 'Do you know?' box explains DIGIPIN, India's 10-character digital address system for every location. The section concludes that nature has no boundaries — air, water, clouds, seeds, and animals move freely across the world, just as people share food, ideas, music, and trade, making Earth a shared home.

tasks.task_text: We all live in this planet and each of us has an address. Fill up your address below. (Name, House number/building name, Street name, Village/Town/City, District, State/Union Territory, Country, Planet)
Use a globe and find out: Are all the oceans on Earth connected with each other?
Use a globe and find out: Where is India on the globe?

sections.title: Story 1: The Travelling Birds!
sections.content_summary: This story introduces rosy starlings, migratory birds that fly thousands of kilometres every winter from Russia, Mongolia, and nearby countries to India. The birds thrive in India's warm climate and help farmers by feeding on locusts and grasshoppers. The story uses this example to show how nature is globally connected — animals cross continents freely, demonstrating that the natural world has no borders.

tasks.task_text: Does the rosy starling visit your area? What is it called locally?
What does this story tell us about nature?
Make a poster of 5 birds that visit your place in winter. Try to find out where they come from.
Using a string, trace the journey on a globe showing the paths rosy starlings take (Russia/Mongolia → India).
Imagine you are a bird travelling the world. Write a short postcard or note about what you see and what helps you on your journey (wind, ocean currents, warm weather). Share it with your classmates.
What does it mean when we say 'nature has no boundaries'?

sections.title: Story 2: Yoga—India's Gift to the World!
sections.content_summary: This section traces the 3,000-year history of yoga as a practice developed in India for bodily health and mental peace, rooted in harmony with oneself and nature. Travellers, scholars, and teachers from India gradually spread yoga across the world, and today it is practised in almost every country. The UN's declaration of 21 June as the International Day of Yoga is highlighted as evidence of yoga's global reach. This story exemplifies how India's cultural-philosophical traditions have become part of the global human heritage.

tasks.task_text: Why do you think yoga became popular in so many countries?
Can you name a yoga pose you have tried or seen? What do you think it helps with?

sections.title: Story 3: Chilli—A Spice that Changed our Lives!
sections.content_summary: The chilli plant originated in South America and was brought to India by Portuguese travellers 400–500 years ago, where it found new soil and climate and became central to Indian cuisine. Before chillies arrived, black pepper was used to add spice to food. The journey of the chilli is presented as an example of how plants travel from distant lands and become embedded in new cultures and cuisines, connecting people across continents through food.

tasks.task_text: What would happen if chillies disappeared from our kitchens for a week?
Ask your parents and write down the name of any recipe in which they have used black pepper and not red chillies.
Using a coloured string, trace the journey of chillies from South America to India on the globe.

Potatoes, tomatoes, peanuts, cashews, and many other foods have similar stories. They all travelled a long way to reach India and now we enjoy these every day in our food. Find out and write the story of any one of them.

sections.title: Story 4: The Sweet Story of Sugar!
sections.content_summary: This story tells how India originated the process of making jaggery from sugarcane juice and later refining it into sugar, a technique that spread globally through trade and travel. Before sugar, people worldwide used honey and other naturally sweet substances. The section also notes that rice, mangoes, and bananas from India spread to other lands, while paper came to India from China through trade. The overall message is that meals carry flavours and stories from around the globe, demonstrating deep global interconnectedness.

tasks.task_text: If you could choose one Indian food to share with the world, what would it be?
What kind of new food items do you think will travel in the future?

sections.title: Story 5: The Mexican Marigold Moves into India!
sections.content_summary: Marigolds originated in Mexico, where they are used in festivals, and travelled across the world to India, where they became deeply embedded in cultural and religious life — seen in temples, homes, weddings, and festivals like Diwali. The story highlights how the flower's vibrant orange and yellow colours resonated with Indian values of warmth, celebration, and spirituality. This is presented as an example of how elements from distant cultures can become inseparable parts of a new culture, illustrating shared human practices around flowers and celebrations.

tasks.task_text: Why do you think both Indian and Mexican cultures use marigolds during their festivals?
Why do you think people in different countries include flowers in their celebrations?

sections.title: Story 6: The Cows that Went to Brazil!
sections.content_summary: Portuguese traders took Indian cows — from the Gir, Kankrej, and Ongole breeds — to Brazil, where these hardy, productive breeds thrived and today supply more than three-fourths of Brazil's milk. These Indian breeds became so significant in Brazil that they appear on stamps and coins. The story demonstrates how animals, like plants and ideas, move across continents and enrich other societies, further illustrating the theme of global interconnection through trade and travel.

tasks.task_text: What does this story tell us about animals moving from one place to another?
With help of your teacher or an elder find out the names of at least 5 different breeds of cows.

Make a list of a few things in your house or school that may have come from another part of the world. Find out where they originally came from. (Items: Clothing, Food, Sports, Musical instruments, Tree)

sections.title: Web of Life
sections.content_summary: This section synthesises all the preceding stories, arguing that they are ultimately about people, journeys, sharing, and cultural blending — not just birds, plants, or objects. It states that whether a bird migrates across continents, a seed grows in new soil, or yoga reaches every corner of the globe, all of it points to one truth: we are all connected. Earth is described as a living web of life where people, animals, plants, and ideas travel, mix, and grow together, and caring for each other and the planet makes us part of that connection.

tasks.task_text: Interview a grandparent or neighbour: Ask them about a food item, song, or custom that was not there in their childhood but is common today. Find out where it came from.

From the stories you have read: List different things that came to India from other countries and those that travelled from India to the world. Then, using two different coloured strings, trace their journeys on a map.

sections.title: One Earth, One Family!
sections.content_summary: This closing section introduces the ancient Indian concept 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' (the world is one family), extending family membership to all people, animals, trees, rivers, air, and sky. It states that Earth is the only known planet with life, having supported living beings for thousands of years, and that it needs care. When humans care for the Earth they are caring for each other, as all belong to the same family. The MoEFCC logo is highlighted as a symbol of the balance between nature and human life and the principle that caring for the environment means caring for ourselves.

tasks.task_text: What does 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' teach us?
Write the meaning of 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' in your own words.
How can we live like one big family, even when we are different?
Pick one of the ideas. Write a short story about how this idea might have travelled from one place to another long ago. Think about how it helped people change their lives or the way they think.
Write one way you can care for the Earth.
Identify of an item that can travel from India to other parts of the world and write about it.

dual_strand.natural: Earth as a shared planetary home — migration of birds, plants, and animals across natural boundaries
dual_strand.human_cultural: Global exchange of ideas, foods, and practices (yoga, chilli, sugar, marigold, Indian cow breeds); the Indian philosophy of Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam as a framework for planetary care
